Notice Of Motion And Motion For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement
Off Calendar Aug-23-2024 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: Appearance via Zoom:
Meg Rosequist, Esq. of Meyers Nave, Tel. 213.327.8702, appearing for the Defendant San Francisco Unified School District;
No appearance by any other party or counsel.

Hearing re: DEFENDANT SAN FRANCISCO UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT'S Motion For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement.

The matter is not contested. No argument is held. Ms. Rosequist appears to ensure that there are no issues. At the conclusio of Ms. Rosequist's comments, the Court adopted its tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant San Francisco Unified School District's ("SFUSD") motion for determination of good faith settlement is granted.

The party contesting the good faith of the settlement has the burden of proof. (CCP 877.6(d) ["The party asserting the lack of good faith shall have the burden of proof on that issue."] Nevertheless, substantial evidence must be present for the court to sanction a "good faith settlement." (Mattco Forge, Inc. v. Arthur Young & Co. (1995) 38 Cal.App.4th 1337, 1350, n.6. [settlors must provide substantial evidence to support a critical assumption as to the nature and extent of its liability].)

When determining whether a settlement was entered into in good faith the court should take into account a rough approximation of plaintiff's total recovery and defendants' proportionate liability, the amount paid in settlement, and recognize that a settlor should pay less in settlement than if he were found liable after trial. The court should also consider the financial condition of the settlor, the existence of insurance, and any evidence of collusion. (Tech-Bilt, Inc. v. Woodward-Clyde & Assoc. (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488, 499.)

Plaintiff's settlement demand claimed $848,108 in damages. (Stone Decl., Ex. B.) But SFUSD cannot be liable for a large majority of those damages. For example, plaintiff seeks approximately $600,000 in punitive damages, but a public entity such as SFUSD cannot be liable for such damages per Government Code section 818. Plaintiff claims general damages of $212,027 based on the Instagram posts and in-school bullying. SFUSD cannot be liable for a large portion of those damages as it cannot be liable for the internet postings under the Communications Decency Act. (47 U.S.C.A Section. 230.) SFUSD also correctly notes that there is no joint liability for non-economic damages (Civil Code section 1431.2) and there is no right of contribution for intentional torts (CCP 875(d)). Given these facts and plaintiff's claimed economic damages of $42,405.40, the $25,000 settlement is "in the ballpark."

The court denies objector's request to deny or continue this motion for further discovery. Plaintiff claimed the above damages on March 8, 2023, and the settlors reached their agreement on June 6, 2023. (Rosequist Decl., par. 9.) The evaluation of the good faith of a settlement must "be made on the basis of information available at the time of settlement." (Tech-Bilt, 38 Cal.3d at p. 499.) There is little point in allowing discovery regarding SFUSD's financial condition since it does not contend that its insolvency is a basis for a discounted settlement. (Cahill v. San Diego Gas & Elec. Co. (2011) 194 Cal.App.4th 939, 968.) The court concludes that substantial evidence supports finding that the settlement was in good faith at the time it was made. Under the present circumstances, prolonging the good faith determination and requiring settlors to incur additional costs would undermine the public policy in favor of settlement. (Id. at 966.)

The Court will review the proposed order submitted by Ms. Rosequist.

Judge: Rochelle C. East. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. Matter Not Reported. Bailiff: Nguyen. (301/RCE)

MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Oct-14-2022 Continued To Nov-18-22 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 302
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT JONATHAN LEE'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Lee's anti-SLAPP motion to strike is continued to November 18, 2022.

Defendant Lee filed his anti-SLAPP motion to strike on August 12, 2022, and that motion attacked the original complaint. On September 1, 2022, the plaintiff filed a first amended complaint. Plaintiff and defendant SFUSD agreed to set a demurrer and motion to strike the first amended complaint for November 18, 2022. The general rule is that an amended complaint supersedes a prior complaint and a motion directed to the prior complaint is moot. (See State Compensation Ins. Fund v. Superior Court (2010) 184 Cal.App.4th 1124, 1131 ["Because there is but one complaint in a civil action [citation], the filing of an amended complaint moots a motion directed to a prior complaint."].) This rule is altered in the context of anti-SLAPP motions. In JKC3H8 v. Colton (2013) 221 Cal.App.4th 468, 477-478, the court explains that:

So too does an amended complaint render moot an anti-SLAPP motion directed to a prior complaint, with the following caveat: A plaintiff or cross-complainant may not seek to subvert or avoid a ruling on an anti-SLAPP motion by amending the challenged complaint or cross-complaint in response to the motion.

In any event, for the sake of judicial resources, it makes sense to have a single hearing on November 18, 2022.

Judge: Vedica Puri; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/VP)
